content.text: Azure Resource Explorer is a web-based tool available at resources.azure.com that acts as a live browser for every resource in your Azure subscriptions. It communicates directly with the Azure Resource Manager (ARM) REST API, presenting the raw JSON state of each resource and letting you make GET, PUT, PATCH, and POST requests — all through the browser, authenticated by your Azure AD session.

Unlike the Azure Portal (which hides ARM complexity behind a polished UI), Resource Explorer exposes the underlying API layer. This makes it invaluable for discovering the exact REST URL, the correct api-version, and the full JSON schema for any resource type before writing automation code.

attrs.alt: Azure Resource Explorer request flow and capabilities

content.text: The left pane of resources.azure.com shows a hierarchical tree that mirrors the ARM scope hierarchy: subscriptions → resource groups → resource providers → resource types → individual resources. Clicking any node loads its JSON representation in the right pane.
Open https://resources.azure.com and sign in with your Azure account.
Expand Subscriptions and select the target subscription.
Expand Resource Groups and drill into the resource group.
Expand providers to see all registered resource providers (e.g. Microsoft.Compute, Microsoft.Storage).
Expand a provider to see its resource types, then click a resource to view live JSON.
As you navigate the tree, the URL bar at the top of the resource pane updates to reflect the exact ARM REST URL. This is the canonical endpoint you would use in curl, az rest, or an SDK call.

# Example URL shown in Resource Explorer for a virtual machine:
https://resources.azure.com/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM

# Breaking it down:
# subscriptions/{id}         — scope
# resourceGroups/{rg}        — resource group
# providers/Microsoft.Compute — resource provider namespace
# virtualMachines/{name}     — resource type and name
The api-version query parameter displayed in Resource Explorer is the version currently supported for that resource type — copy it directly into your ARM REST calls to avoid version guessing.

By default Resource Explorer opens in Read Only mode, showing the GET response for each resource. The JSON displayed is the identical payload returned by the ARM REST API — no formatting or data transformation.
Copying the Equivalent az rest Command
The Resource Explorer UI shows the ARM REST URL and api-version, making it straightforward to reproduce the same call with az rest or curl.

# Reproduce the Resource Explorer GET in az CLI
az rest   --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM?api-version=2023-09-01"

# Or with curl (requires a bearer token)
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM?api-version=2023-09-01" | jq .

Switching Resource Explorer to Read/Write mode (toggle in the top toolbar) enables PUT, PATCH, and POST operations against the live resource. This is useful for testing API calls before committing them to scripts.

In the Resource Explorer toolbar:
  1. Click the "Read Only" toggle → switches to "Read/Write"
  2. A warning banner appears: "Changes made here affect your live Azure resources"
  3. The JSON editor pane becomes editable
  4. PUT / PATCH / POST buttons appear above the resource pane

# Equivalent PATCH via az rest
az rest   --method PATCH   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM?api-version=2023-09-01"   --body '{"tags": {"environment": "staging", "owner": "platform-team"}}'
Always test PATCH and PUT operations on a non-production resource first. Resource Explorer does not have an undo — a PUT replaces the entire resource definition if the body is incomplete.
Discovering Resource Providers and API Versions

One of the most practical uses of Resource Explorer is finding the exact namespace, resource type name, and current api-version for a resource — information that is scattered across docs but always accurate in Resource Explorer.

# List all registered providers in a subscription
az provider list --query "[].namespace" -o tsv | sort

# Check a specific provider's resource types and api-versions
az provider show   --namespace Microsoft.Compute   --query "resourceTypes[].{Type:resourceType, Versions:apiVersions[0]}"   --output table

In the Resource Explorer UI, click a resource and look at the URL bar — the query string includes the api-version that ARM is using for that resource type right now. This version is guaranteed to be valid for GET; it may not be the only version supported for write operations, but it is a safe starting point.

Resource Explorer can export the ARM template representation of any visible resource. This captures the resource's current configuration in a format that can be deployed with az deployment group create or imported into a Bicep file via decompilation.
Navigate to a resource group or individual resource in the tree.
Click 'Export template' in the action bar (above the JSON pane).
Resource Explorer calls the /exportTemplate ARM action and displays the resulting JSON.
Copy the template JSON or download it as a .json file.
Converting Exported ARM Template to Bicep

# Save the exported JSON
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/exportTemplate?api-version=2021-04-01"   -X POST   -H "Content-Type: application/json"   -d '{"resources": ["*"], "options": "IncludeParameterDefaultValue"}' > exported.json

# Decompile to Bicep
az bicep decompile --file exported.json

# This produces exported.bicep — review and clean up generated code
# (decompiled Bicep often requires manual cleanup for complex resources)
Exported ARM templates may include hardcoded resource IDs and location values. Parameterize these before using the template for repeatable deployments.

Many Azure resources have sub-resources and nested properties that are not obvious from the Portal UI. Resource Explorer exposes the full hierarchy — for example, a virtual network contains subnets, route tables, and NSG associations as child nodes in the tree.

Example tree for a virtual network (Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/myVNet):

  myVNet/
  ├── subnets/
  │   ├── default          → GET /virtualNetworks/myVNet/subnets/default
  │   └── backendSubnet    → GET /virtualNetworks/myVNet/subnets/backendSubnet
  ├── virtualNetworkPeerings/
  │   └── peer-to-hub      → GET /virtualNetworks/myVNet/virtualNetworkPeerings/peer-to-hub
  └── usages               → GET /virtualNetworks/myVNet/usages (action, not a child resource)

# Get a specific subnet directly via az rest
az rest   --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/myVNet/subnets/default?api-version=2023-09-01"

# List all subnets in a VNet
az rest   --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/myVNet/subnets?api-version=2023-09-01"   | jq '.value[] | {name: .name, prefix: .properties.addressPrefix}'
Using Resource Explorer to Debug ARM Deployments
When an ARM or Bicep deployment fails, Resource Explorer helps diagnose the issue by showing the current state of partially-deployed resources and the exact error from ARM.

# List deployments in a resource group
az deployment group list   --resource-group myRG   --query "[].{Name:name, State:properties.provisioningState}"   --output table

# Get the failed operation details from a deployment
az deployment operation group list   --resource-group myRG   --name main   --query "[?properties.provisioningState=='Failed'].{Resource:properties.targetResource.resourceType, Error:properties.statusMessage.error}"   --output json
Inspecting Partial State in Resource Explorer

After a partial failure, navigate to the resource group in Resource Explorer to see which resources reached provisioningState: Succeeded and which are stuck in Updating or Failed. The live JSON shows the exact error codes that ARM returned.

# Read the provisioningState for all resources in a RG via az CLI
az resource list   --resource-group myRG   --query "[].{Name:name, Type:type, State:properties.provisioningState}"   --output table

# Deep-read a specific failed resource
az rest   --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM?api-version=2023-09-01"   | jq '.properties | {provisioningState, vmId, statusCode: .instanceView.statuses}'
Resource Explorer vs Other Exploration Tools
Interactive live JSON browsing and API discovery
Visual resource management and guided workflows
Scripted one-off REST calls with full control
Production automation with typed models and retries
Capturing current config for IaC migration
Workflow 1: Discover the API Before Writing Code
Create a test resource in the Portal (e.g. a storage account).
Open Resource Explorer, navigate to the storage account.
Read the JSON — identify which properties you need to set in automation.
Note the ARM URL and api-version shown in the URL bar.
Translate the JSON body into a Bicep resource block or SDK call.

# Dump configuration of two storage accounts to files
az rest --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/storageA?api-version=2023-01-01"   | jq '.properties' > storageA.json

az rest --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/storageB?api-version=2023-01-01"   | jq '.properties' > storageB.json

# Diff the two configurations
diff <(jq -S . storageA.json) <(jq -S . storageB.json)

import subprocess, json

def get_resource(sub_id, rg, provider, res_type, res_name, api_version):
    """Fetch a resource's full JSON via az rest."""
    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{sub_id}"
        f"/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/{provider}/{res_type}/{res_name}"
        f"?api-version={api_version}"
    )
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["az", "rest", "--method", "GET", "--url", url],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
    )
    return json.loads(result.stdout)

vm = get_resource(
    sub_id="00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000",
    rg="myRG",
    provider="Microsoft.Compute",
    res_type="virtualMachines",
    res_name="myVM",
    api_version="2023-09-01"
)

print(f"VM size: {vm['properties']['hardwareProfile']['vmSize']}")
print(f"OS type: {vm['properties']['storageProfile']['osDisk']['osType']}")
print(f"State:   {vm['properties']['provisioningState']}")
Understanding ARM Resource Properties in Depth

The JSON payload returned by ARM for any resource divides into a standard envelope and a resource-specific properties object. Understanding this structure helps you write accurate Bicep and SDK code the first time.
Resource name within its type scope

# Check provisioningState for all VMs in a subscription
az rest   --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines?api-version=2023-09-01"   | jq '.value[] | {name: .name, state: .properties.provisioningState, size: .properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize}'

# Same with az CLI shorthand
az vm list --query "[].{Name:name, State:provisioningState, Size:hardwareProfile.vmSize}" --output table

# Get creation and last-modified metadata for a storage account
az rest   --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/myStorage?api-version=2023-01-01"   | jq '{name: .name, created: .systemData.createdAt, createdBy: .systemData.createdBy, lastModified: .systemData.lastModifiedAt}'
Automating Bulk Reads with Resource Explorer Patterns

The REST patterns you discover in Resource Explorer translate directly into bulk automation scripts. Two common patterns are list-then-get (for resources that need detail fetching per item) and top-level list (for resources that include full detail in the collection response).
Pattern 1: List Collection Then Read Each Item

import subprocess, json

def az_rest(url):
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["az", "rest", "--method", "GET", "--url", url],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
    )
    return json.loads(result.stdout)

sub_id = "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"

# Step 1: list all storage accounts in the subscription
accounts_url = (
    f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{sub_id}"
    "/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts?api-version=2023-01-01"
)
accounts = az_rest(accounts_url)["value"]

# Step 2: for each account, read the blob service properties
for acct in accounts:
    blob_url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com{acct['id']}"
        "/blobServices/default?api-version=2023-01-01"
    )
    blob_props = az_rest(blob_url)
    versioning = blob_props["properties"].get("isVersioningEnabled", False)
    soft_delete = blob_props["properties"]["deleteRetentionPolicy"].get("enabled", False)
    print(f"{acct['name']:40s}  versioning={versioning}  soft-delete={soft_delete}")

# List only storage accounts of kind StorageV2 in eastus
az rest   --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts?api-version=2023-01-01"   | jq '[.value[] | select(.kind == "StorageV2" and .location == "eastus") | {name: .name, rg: (.id | split("/")[4])}]'

# List VMs with a specific tag value
az rest   --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines?api-version=2023-09-01"   | jq '[.value[] | select(.tags.environment == "production") | {name: .name, size: .properties.hardwareProfile.vmSize}]'

import subprocess, json

def az_rest_paginated(url):
    """Follow nextLink pagination to collect all results."""
    all_items = []
    while url:
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["az", "rest", "--method", "GET", "--url", url],
            capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
        )
        page = json.loads(result.stdout)
        all_items.extend(page.get("value", []))
        url = page.get("nextLink")  # None when last page
    return all_items

sub_id = "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
vms = az_rest_paginated(
    f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{sub_id}"
    "/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines?api-version=2023-09-01"
)
print(f"Total VMs across all resource groups: {len(vms)}")

The providers endpoint in ARM returns metadata about each resource provider: which resource types it supports, which api-versions are available for each, and which Azure regions support each resource type. Resource Explorer exposes this at the /providers level of the tree.

# Get all resource types and their latest api-versions for a provider
az rest   --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/providers/Microsoft.Compute?api-version=2021-04-01"   | jq '.resourceTypes[] | {type: .resourceType, latestApi: .apiVersions[0], locations: (.locations | length | tostring) + " regions"}'

# Check if a resource type is available in a specific region
az rest   --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/providers/Microsoft.ContainerService?api-version=2021-04-01"   | jq '.resourceTypes[] | select(.resourceType == "managedClusters") | {apiVersions: .apiVersions[:3], zones: .zoneMappings | length}'

import subprocess, json, re

def get_latest_stable_api_version(sub_id, provider, resource_type):
    """Return the newest stable (non-preview) api-version for a resource type."""
    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{sub_id}"
        f"/providers/{provider}?api-version=2021-04-01"
    )
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["az", "rest", "--method", "GET", "--url", url],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
    )
    data = json.loads(result.stdout)

    for rt in data["resourceTypes"]:
        if rt["resourceType"].lower() == resource_type.lower():
            stable = [v for v in rt["apiVersions"] if "preview" not in v.lower()]
            return stable[0] if stable else rt["apiVersions"][0]
    return None

sub = "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
version = get_latest_stable_api_version(sub, "Microsoft.Storage", "storageAccounts")
print(f"Latest stable api-version: {version}")
Security Considerations When Using Resource Explorer

Resource Explorer authenticates using your Azure AD credentials via the browser session. The permissions it shows and the operations it allows are scoped to exactly what your RBAC role grants — it does not elevate permissions or bypass Azure Policy.
Browser session uses your Azure AD token (OAuth 2.0)
Use MFA; avoid using Resource Explorer on shared machines
PATCH/PUT/POST operations hit live resources with no undo
Test write operations on dev/staging resources first
Properties may include connection strings or keys in plain text
Use Key Vault references in resource configs; rotate keys if exposed
Resource IDs, subscription IDs, and hostnames are visible
Redact before sharing screenshots of Resource Explorer
Full ARM URLs (including resource IDs) are saved in history
Use InPrivate/Incognito mode for sensitive subscription browsing
Resource Explorer is a read/write tool that operates on live Azure resources. Treat it like direct database access — understand what you are changing before clicking PUT or PATCH.

Azure Resource Explorer at resources.azure.com is the most direct way to inspect ARM's live view of any resource. It reveals the exact JSON schema, REST endpoint, and api-version for every resource type — information that feeds directly into az rest calls, Bicep templates, and SDK automation. Use it in Read/Write mode to prototype API calls before committing them to scripts, and use the exported templates as a starting point for IaC migrations.
